// Event flow is the order in which event is received on the web page. When you click an element that is nested in various other elements, before your click actually reaches its destination, or target element, it must trigger the click event for each of its parent elements first, starting at the top with the global window object.
//     There are two ways of event flow

//     1. Top to Bottom(Event Capturing)
//     2. Bottom to Top (Event Bubbling)

// Event bubbling is a type of event propagation where the event first triggers on the innermost target element, and then successively triggers on the ancestors (parents) of the target element in the same nesting hierarchy till it reaches the outermost DOM element.
// Bubbling means that the event propagates from the target element (i.e. the button the user clicked) up through its ancestor tree, starting from the nearest one. By default, all events bubble.

// Event capturing is a type of event propagation where the event is first captured by the outermost element, and then successively triggers on the descendants (children) of the target element in the same nesting hierarchy till it reaches the innermost DOM element.


When an event occurs on a DOM element, that event does not entirely occur on that just one element. 

In the Bubbling Phase, the event bubbles up or it goes to its parent, to its grandparents, to its grandparent's parent until it reaches all the way to the window.

while in the Capturing Phase the event starts from the window down to the element that triggered the event or the event.target.

Event Propagation has three phases.

**Capturing Phase** – the event starts from window then goes down to every element until it reaches the target element.
**Target Phase** – the event has reached the target element.
**Bubbling Phase** – the event bubbles up from the target element then goes up every element until it reaches the window.

![alt text](image.png)
```html
<html>
  <body>
    <div id="btn-container">
      <button class="btn">Click me</button>
    </div>
  </body>
</html>


const ancestors = [
  window, document, document.documentElement,
  document.body, document.getElementById('btn-container')
];

```

// If we add an event listener to each element in the tree, as shown above, we would see a listener fired by the button first, then each one of the others firing from the nearest ancestor all the way up to Window.

// Target phase
document.querySelector('.btn').addEventListener('click', e => {
  console.log(`Hello from ${e.target}`);
});
// Bubble phase
ancestors.forEach(a => {
  a.addEventListener('click', e => {
    console.log(`Hello from ${e.currentTarget}`);
  });
});

// Capturing is the exact opposite of bubbling, meaning that the outer event handlers are fired before the most specific handler (i.e. the one on the button). Note that all capturing event handlers are run first, then all the bubbling event handlers. 
ancestors.forEach(a => {
    a.addEventListener('click', e => {
      console.log(`Hello from ${e.currentTarget}`);
    }, true);
  });


  window.addEventListener('click', e => {
    if (e.target.className === 'btn') console.log('Hello there!');
  });


//   Event propagation
// Having explained event bubbling and capturing, we can now explain the three phases of event propagation:

// During the capture phase, the event starts from Window and moves down to Document, the root element and through ancestors of the target element.
// During the target phase, the event gets triggered on the event target (e.g. the button the user clicked).
// During the bubble phase, the event bubbles up through ancestors of the target element until the root element, Document and, finally, Window.


const addEventListenerAll = (targets, type, listener, options, useCapture) => {
    targets.forEach(target =>
      target.addEventListener(type, listener, options, useCapture)
    );
  };
  
  addEventListenerAll(document.querySelectorAll('a'), 'click', () =>
    console.log('Clicked a link')
  );
  // Logs 'Clicked a link' whenever any anchor element is clicked


  const removeEventListenerAll = (
    targets,
    type,
    listener,
    options,
    useCapture
  ) => {
    targets.forEach(target =>
      target.removeEventListener(type, listener, options, useCapture)
    );
  };
  
  const linkListener = () => console.log('Clicked a link');
  document.querySelector('a').addEventListener('click', linkListener);
  removeEventListenerAll(document.querySelectorAll('a'), 'click', linkListener);


  const addMultipleListeners = (el, types, listener, options, useCapture) => {
    types.forEach(type =>
      el.addEventListener(type, listener, options, useCapture)
    );
  };
  
  addMultipleListeners(
    document.querySelector('.my-element'),
    ['click', 'mousedown'],
    () => { console.log('hello!') }
  );



**What's Event Bubbling?**
↑ When an event occurs on a DOM element, that event does not entirely occur on that just one element. In the Bubbling Phase, the event bubbles up or it goes to its parent, to its grandparents, to its grandparent's parent until it reaches all the way to the window.

If we have an example markup like this.

```html
 <div class="grandparent">
    <div class="parent">
      <div class="child">1</div>
    </div>
  </div>

```
And our js code.

```js
function addEvent(el, event, callback, isCapture = false) {
  if (!el || !event || !callback || typeof callback !== 'function') return;
  if (typeof el === 'string') {
    el = document.querySelector(el);
  };
  el.addEventListener(event, callback, isCapture);
}

addEvent(document, 'DOMContentLoaded', () => {
  const child = document.querySelector('.child');
  const parent = document.querySelector('.parent');
  const grandparent = document.querySelector('.grandparent');

  addEvent(child, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('child');
  });

  addEvent(parent, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('parent');
  });

  addEvent(grandparent, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('grandparent');
  });

  addEvent(document, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('document');
  });

  addEvent('html', 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('html');
  })

  addEvent(window, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('window');
  })

});

```

The addEventListener method has a third optional parameter useCapture with a default value of false the event will occur in the Bubbling phase if true the event will occur in the Capturing Phase. If we click on the child element it logs child,parent,grandparent, html, document and window respectively on the console. This is Event Bubbling.


**What's Event Capturing?**
↑ When an event occurs on a DOM element, that event does not entirely occur on that just one element. In Capturing Phase, the event starts from the window all the way down to the element that triggered the event.

If we have an example markup like this.
```html
 <div class="grandparent">
    <div class="parent">
      <div class="child">1</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  ```
And our js code.
```js
function addEvent(el, event, callback, isCapture = false) {
  if (!el || !event || !callback || typeof callback !== 'function') return;
  if (typeof el === 'string') {
    el = document.querySelector(el);
  };
  el.addEventListener(event, callback, isCapture);
}

addEvent(document, 'DOMContentLoaded', () => {
  const child = document.querySelector('.child');
  const parent = document.querySelector('.parent');
  const grandparent = document.querySelector('.grandparent');

  addEvent(child, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('child');
  }, true);

  addEvent(parent, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('parent');
  }, true);

  addEvent(grandparent, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('grandparent');
  }, true);

  addEvent(document, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('document');
  }, true);

  addEvent('html', 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('html');
  }, true)

  addEvent(window, 'click', function (e) {
    console.log('window');
  }, true)

});

```
The addEventListener method has a third optional parameter useCapture with a default value of false the event will occur in the Bubbling phase if true the event will occur in the Capturing Phase. 

If we click on the child element it logs window,document,html, grandparent and parent and child respectively on the console. This is Event Capturing.


**What's the difference between event.preventDefault() and event.stopPropagation() methods?**
- ↑ The event.preventDefault() method prevents the default behavior of an element. 

- If used in a form element it prevents it from submitting. If used in an anchor element it prevents it from navigating. 

- If used in a contextmenu it prevents it from showing or displaying. 

- While the event.stopPropagation() method stops the propogation of an event or it stops the event from occurring in the bubbling or capturing phase.

# Event Flow in JavaScript & React

**Event Flow** describes how an event travels through the DOM when a user interacts with an element.

Example:

&lt;div id="grandParent"&gt;&nbsp; &lt;div id="parent"&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; &lt;button id="child"&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; Click Me&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; &lt;/button&gt;&nbsp; &lt;/div&gt;&lt;/div&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

DOM Tree:

```text
grandParent
     ↓
   parent
     ↓
   child
```

***

# 3 Phases of Event Flow

```text
1. Capturing Phase
2. Target Phase
3. Bubbling Phase
```

***

# Event Flow Diagram

```text
Window
  ↓
Document
  ↓
HTML
  ↓
BODY
  ↓
grandParent
  ↓
parent
  ↓
child

(Target)

child
  ↑
parent
  ↑
grandParent
  ↑
BODY
  ↑
HTML
```

***

# 1. Capturing Phase

Event travels from:

```text
Top → Bottom
```

```text
Window
 ↓
Document
 ↓
grandParent
 ↓
parent
 ↓
child
```

To listen during capturing:

```javascript
element.addEventListener(
  "click",
  handler,
  true
);
```

Notice:

```javascript
true
```

enables capture mode.

***

## Example

&lt;div id="parent"&gt;&nbsp; &lt;button id="child"&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; Click&nbsp; &lt;/button&gt;&lt;/div&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

```javascript
document
  .getElementById("parent")
  .addEventListener(
    "click",
    () => {
      console.log(
        "Parent Capture"
      );
    },
    true
  );

document
  .getElementById("child")
  .addEventListener(
    "click",
    () => {
      console.log(
        "Child Click"
      );
    }
  );
```

Output:

```text
Parent Capture
Child Click
```

***

# 2. Target Phase

Event reaches:

```text
Actual Element Clicked
```

Example:

```javascript
<button>
```

User clicks:

```text
Target = Button
```

***

# 3. Bubbling Phase

Default browser behavior.

Event travels:

```text
Bottom → Top
```

```text
child
 ↑
parent
 ↑
grandParent
```

***

## Example

&lt;div id="parent"&gt;&nbsp; &lt;button id="child"&gt;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; Click&nbsp; &lt;/button&gt;&lt;/div&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

```javascript
parent.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () => {
    console.log("Parent");
  }
);

child.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () => {
    console.log("Child");
  }
);
```

Output:

```text
Child
Parent
```

***

# Complete Example

```javascript
grandParent.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () =>
    console.log(
      "GrandParent Capture"
    ),
  true
);

parent.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () =>
    console.log(
      "Parent Capture"
    ),
  true
);

child.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () =>
    console.log("Child")
);

parent.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () =>
    console.log(
      "Parent Bubble"
    )
);

grandParent.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () =>
    console.log(
      "GrandParent Bubble"
    )
);
```

Output:

```text
GrandParent Capture
Parent Capture
Child
Parent Bubble
GrandParent Bubble
```

***

# stopPropagation()

Stops event movement.

```javascript
child.addEventListener(
  "click",
  event => {

    event.stopPropagation();

    console.log("Child");
  }
);
```

Output:

```text
Child
```

Parent never executes.

***

# Event Delegation (Very Important)

Instead of:

```text
1000 buttons
1000 listeners
```

Use:

```text
1 Parent Listener
```

***

## Example

&lt;ul id="users"&gt;&nbsp; &lt;li&gt;Sudhir&lt;/li&gt;&nbsp; &lt;li&gt;John&lt;/li&gt;&nbsp; &lt;li&gt;David&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

```javascript
document
  .getElementById("users")
  .addEventListener(
    "click",
    event => {

      console.log(
        event.target
          .textContent
      );

    }
  );
```

Single listener handles all items.

***

# React Event Flow

React uses:

```text
Synthetic Events
```

and event delegation internally. React DOM contains an event registry and delegated event handling infrastructure. [\[persistent...epoint.com\]](https://persistentsystems.sharepoint.com/sites/PersistentLearningandDevelopment/Propeller%20Program/Batch%2024/Propeller%20Group%201/codebase/corporate-learning-system-main/frontend/node_modules/react-dom/umd/react-dom.development.js?web=1)

***

## Example

```jsx
function App() {

  return (

    <div
      onClick={() =>
        console.log(
          "Parent"
        )
      }
    >

      <button
        onClick={() =>
          console.log(
            "Button"
          )
        }
      >
        Click
      </button>

    </div>

  );
}
```

Output:

```text
Button
Parent
```

React follows bubbling by default.

***

# React stopPropagation()

```jsx
<button
  onClick={(e) => {

    e.stopPropagation();

    console.log(
      "Button"
    );
  }}
>
  Click
</button>
```

Output:

```text
Button
```

***

# React Capture Phase

React provides capture handlers.

```jsx
<div
  onClickCapture={() =>
    console.log(
      "Capture"
    )
  }

  onClick={() =>
    console.log(
      "Bubble"
    )
  }
>
  <button>
    Click
  </button>
</div>
```

Output:

```text
Capture
Bubble
```

***

# Interview Questions

### Difference Between Capturing and Bubbling?

**Capturing**

```text
Top → Bottom
```

**Bubbling**

```text
Bottom → Top
```

***

### Default Phase?

```text
Bubbling
```

***

### How to Enable Capture?

```javascript
addEventListener(
  "click",
  handler,
  true
);
```

or in React:

```jsx
onClickCapture
```

***

### Why Event Delegation?

```text
Less Memory
Better Performance
Handles Dynamic Elements
```

***

# Senior React Interview Answer

Event Flow consists of:

```text
Capturing Phase
      ↓
Target Phase
      ↓
Bubbling Phase
```

Browsers execute event handlers during bubbling by default. React implements a synthetic event system and uses delegated event handling so that events can be managed efficiently across components. In React, `onClick` participates in bubbling, `onClickCapture` participates in capturing, and `stopPropagation()` prevents the event from continuing through the event flow. This mechanism is the foundation of event delegation patterns used in large React applications. [\[persistent...epoint.com\]](https://persistentsystems.sharepoint.com/sites/PersistentLearningandDevelopment/Propeller%20Program/Batch%2024/Propeller%20Group%201/codebase/corporate-learning-system-main/frontend/node_modules/react-dom/umd/react-dom.development.js?web=1)



# Event Delegation Benefits in React

React uses a delegated event system internally rather than attaching native listeners directly to every element. This helps React handle events efficiently across large component trees.

## Why Event Delegation?

Imagine rendering 1,000 list items.

### ❌ Without Event Delegation

```jsx
{users.map(user => (
  <button
    key={user.id}
    onClick={() =>
      console.log(user.name)
    }
  >
    {user.name}
  </button>
))}
```

Conceptually:

```text
1000 Buttons
1000 Event Handlers
```

***

### ✅ With Event Delegation

Attach one handler to the parent.

```jsx
function UserList() {
  const users = [
    { id: 1, name: "Sudhir" },
    { id: 2, name: "John" }
  ];

  const handleClick = (e) => {
    const item =
      e.target.closest("[data-id]");

    if (!item) return;

    console.log(
      item.dataset.id
    );
  };

  return (
    <div onClick={handleClick}>
      {users.map(user => (
        <button
          key={user.id}
          data-id={user.id}
        >
          {user.name}
        </button>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}
```

Benefits:

```text
✅ Lower memory usage
✅ Better performance
✅ Works with dynamically added items
✅ Easier maintenance
✅ Fewer event listeners
```

***

# Event Delegation Example (Table Rows)

```jsx
function UserTable() {

  const users = [
    { id: 1, name: "Sudhir" },
    { id: 2, name: "John" }
  ];

  const handleTableClick =
    (e) => {

      const row =
        e.target.closest("tr");

      if (!row) return;

      console.log(
        "Selected:",
        row.dataset.userid
      );
    };

  return (
    <table onClick={handleTableClick}>
      <tbody>
        {users.map(user => (
          <tr
            key={user.id}
            data-userid={user.id}
          >
            <td>{user.name}</td>
          </tr>
        ))}
      </tbody>
    </table>
  );
}
```

***

# stopPropagation in React

Event propagation means an event travels up the DOM tree through the bubbling phase. React's `onClick` participates in bubbling by default.

***

## Without stopPropagation

```jsx
function App() {

  return (

    <div
      onClick={() =>
        console.log("Parent")
      }
    >

      <button
        onClick={() =>
          console.log("Button")
        }
      >
        Click
      </button>

    </div>
  );
}
```

### Output

```text
Button
Parent
```

Because:

```text
Button Click
      ↓
Bubble Up
      ↓
Parent Click
```

***

## With stopPropagation

```jsx
function App() {

  const handleButtonClick =
    (e) => {

      e.stopPropagation();

      console.log(
        "Button"
      );
    };

  return (

    <div
      onClick={() =>
        console.log("Parent")
      }
    >

      <button
        onClick={
          handleButtonClick
        }
      >
        Click
      </button>

    </div>
  );
}
```

### Output

```text
Button
```

Now the event stops at:

```text
Button
```

and never reaches:

```text
Parent
```

***

# Real-World React Example

### Card with Inner Actions

```jsx
function UserCard() {

  const openProfile =
    () => {
      console.log(
        "Open Profile"
      );
    };

  const deleteUser =
    (e) => {

      e.stopPropagation();

      console.log(
        "Delete User"
      );
    };

  return (

    <div
      className="card"
      onClick={openProfile}
    >

      <h3>Sudhir</h3>

      <button
        onClick={deleteUser}
      >
        Delete
      </button>

    </div>
  );
}
```

### Without stopPropagation

```text
Delete User
Open Profile
```

### With stopPropagation

```text
Delete User
```

Only the button action executes.

***

# Interview Answer

### When should you use Event Delegation?

```text
Large Lists
Tables
Dynamic Content
Menus
File Explorers
Tree Views
```

### When should you use stopPropagation?

```text
Nested Buttons
Cards with Actions
Modals
Dropdown Menus
Context Menus
Prevent Parent Click Events
```

### One-line Senior React Answer

> Event delegation improves performance by handling events through a common parent instead of attaching listeners to every child. `stopPropagation()` prevents an event from continuing through the bubbling phase, which is useful when child components should handle an interaction without triggering parent handlers. React supports bubbling by default and provides a synthetic event system to manage events efficiently.



These three are frequently asked together in JavaScript and React interviews.

# 1. `event.stopPropagation()`

Stops the event from moving to parent elements.

## Example

```jsx
function App() {

  return (
    <div
      onClick={() =>
        console.log("Parent")
      }
    >
      <button
        onClick={(e) => {

          e.stopPropagation();

          console.log("Button");
        }}
      >
        Click
      </button>
    </div>
  );
}
```

### Output

```text
Button
```

Without:

```javascript
e.stopPropagation();
```

Output:

```text
Button
Parent
```

### What it Stops

```text
Child
  ↑
Parent
  ↑
GrandParent

❌ Stops bubbling further
```

***

# 2. `event.stopImmediatePropagation()`

Stops:

✅ Event bubbling

✅ Other handlers on the SAME element

This is a native DOM API.

***

## Example

```javascript
const button =
  document.getElementById(
    "btn"
  );

button.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () => {
    console.log("Handler 1");
  }
);

button.addEventListener(
  "click",
  (event) => {

    event.stopImmediatePropagation();

    console.log("Handler 2");
  }
);

button.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () => {
    console.log("Handler 3");
  }
);
```

### Output

```text
Handler 1
Handler 2
```

### Why?

```text
Handler 3 never executes
```

because:

```javascript
stopImmediatePropagation()
```

blocks remaining handlers on the same element.

***

## Difference

### stopPropagation()

```text
Stops Parent Handlers

BUT

Same Element Handlers Continue
```

### stopImmediatePropagation()

```text
Stops Parent Handlers

AND

Stops Remaining Handlers
on Current Element
```

***

# 3. `event.preventDefault()`

Prevents the browser's default behaviour.

***

## Link Example

### Without preventDefault

```jsx
<a href="https://google.com">
  Open Google
</a>
```

Browser:

```text
Navigates to Google
```

***

### With preventDefault

```jsx
<a
  href="https://google.com"
  onClick={(e) => {

    e.preventDefault();

    console.log(
      "Custom Logic"
    );
  }}
>
  Open Google
</a>
```

### Output

```text
Custom Logic
```

Browser does NOT navigate.

***

# Form Example

Very common React interview question.

```jsx
function LoginForm() {

  const handleSubmit =
    (e) => {

      e.preventDefault();

      console.log(
        "Form Submitted"
      );
    };

  return (

    <form
      onSubmit={
        handleSubmit
      }
    >

      <button>
        Submit
      </button>

    </form>

  );
}
```

### Without preventDefault

```text
Page Reload
```

### With preventDefault

```text
No Reload
Custom Submit Logic
```

***

# Visual Comparison

## stopPropagation

```text
Child
 ↑
Parent

Stops Here
```

***

## stopImmediatePropagation

```text
Button

Handler 1 ✅

Handler 2 ✅
  ↓

Stops

Handler 3 ❌
Parent ❌
```

***

## preventDefault

```text
Click Link

Default Action
↓
Navigate

preventDefault()
↓
Navigation Cancelled
```

***

# React Example Combining All Three

```jsx
function App() {

  const handleClick =
    (e) => {

      e.preventDefault();

      e.stopPropagation();

      console.log(
        "Button Clicked"
      );
    };

  return (

    <div
      onClick={() =>
        console.log("Parent")
      }
    >

      /home
        Home
      </a>

    </div>

  );
}
```

Output:

```text
Button Clicked
```

Effects:

```text
✅ Parent not triggered

✅ Navigation prevented

✅ Custom logic executed
```

***

# Interview Summary

| Method                       | Stops Bubbling | Stops Same Element Handlers | Prevents Default Browser Action |
| ---------------------------- | -------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| `stopPropagation()`          | ✅              | ❌                           | ❌                               |
| `stopImmediatePropagation()` | ✅              | ✅                           | ❌                               |
| `preventDefault()`           | ❌              | ❌                           | ✅                               |

### Senior React Interview Answer

```text
stopPropagation()
→ Stops the event from bubbling to parent elements.

stopImmediatePropagation()
→ Stops bubbling and prevents any remaining handlers on the same element from executing.

preventDefault()
→ Prevents the browser's default action such as form submission, link navigation, or context menu display.
```

**Easy memory trick:**

```text
stopPropagation
= Stop Parent

stopImmediatePropagation
= Stop Parent + Stop Siblings

preventDefault
= Stop Browser
```


# 1. React Example Using `stopImmediatePropagation()`

⚠️ In React, `stopImmediatePropagation()` is **not directly exposed** on React's SyntheticEvent. To use it, you must access the native event.

## Example

```jsx
function App() {

  const handleClick1 = () => {
    console.log("Handler 1");
  };

  const handleClick2 = (e) => {

    e.nativeEvent.stopImmediatePropagation();

    console.log("Handler 2");
  };

  return (
    <button
      onClick={handleClick1}
      onMouseDown={handleClick2}
    >
      Click Me
    </button>
  );
}
```

***

## More Realistic DOM Example

```jsx
import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";

function App() {

  const btnRef = useRef();

  useEffect(() => {

    const btn = btnRef.current;

    btn.addEventListener(
      "click",
      () => {
        console.log("First");
      }
    );

    btn.addEventListener(
      "click",
      (e) => {

        e.stopImmediatePropagation();

        console.log("Second");
      }
    );

    btn.addEventListener(
      "click",
      () => {
        console.log("Third");
      }
    );

  }, []);

  return (
    <button ref={btnRef}>
      Click
    </button>
  );
}
```

### Output

```text
First
Second
```

```text
Third ❌
```

because:

```javascript
stopImmediatePropagation()
```

stops all remaining handlers on the same element.

***

# 2. When to Use `preventDefault()` in Forms

This is one of the most common React interview questions.

## Without preventDefault

```jsx
function LoginForm() {

  const handleSubmit = () => {
    console.log("Submit");
  };

  return (
    <form onSubmit={handleSubmit}>
      <button>
        Submit
      </button>
    </form>
  );
}
```

### Behaviour

```text
Submit
↓
Page Reload
```

Browser performs its default action.

***

## With preventDefault

```jsx
function LoginForm() {

  const handleSubmit = (e) => {

    e.preventDefault();

    console.log(
      "Custom Submit"
    );
  };

  return (
    <form onSubmit={handleSubmit}>
      <button>
        Submit
      </button>
    </form>
  );
}
```

### Behaviour

```text
Submit
↓
No Page Refresh
↓
AJAX/API Call
↓
React State Update
```

***

## Real React Example

```jsx
function LoginForm() {

  const handleSubmit =
    async (e) => {

      e.preventDefault();

      const result =
        await loginApi();

      console.log(result);
    };

  return (
    <form
      onSubmit={handleSubmit}
    >
      <button>
        Login
      </button>
    </form>
  );
}
```

Used for:

```text
✅ Login Forms
✅ Registration Forms
✅ Search Forms
✅ Contact Forms
✅ API Submissions
```

***

# 3. stopPropagation vs preventDefault

Many developers confuse these.

***

## stopPropagation()

### Purpose

```text
Stop Event Bubbling
```

### Example

```jsx
<div
  onClick={() =>
    console.log("Parent")
  }
>

  <button
    onClick={(e) => {

      e.stopPropagation();

      console.log("Child");

    }}
  >
    Click
  </button>

</div>
```

### Output

```text
Child
```

Parent never executes.

***

## preventDefault()

### Purpose

```text
Stop Browser Default Action
```

### Example

```jsx
/home => {

    e.preventDefault();

    console.log(
      "Navigation Blocked"
    );
  }}
>
  Home
</a>
```

### Output

```text
Navigation Blocked
```

Browser does not navigate.

***

# Visual Difference

## stopPropagation

```text
Button Click
      ↑
Parent Click

stopPropagation()

Button ✅
Parent ❌
```

***

## preventDefault

```text
Anchor Click
      ↓
Navigate

preventDefault()

Click ✅
Navigate ❌
```

***

# Combined Example

```jsx
function Card() {

  return (

    <div
      onClick={() =>
        console.log(
          "Open Profile"
        )
      }
    >

      /delete => {

          e.preventDefault();

          e.stopPropagation();

          console.log(
            "Delete User"
          );

        }}
      >
        Delete
      </a>

    </div>

  );
}
```

### Output

```text
Delete User
```

Effects:

```text
✅ Link does not navigate
✅ Parent click does not execute
```

***

# Interview Comparison Table

| Method                       | Stops Bubbling | Stops Default Action | Stops Other Handlers on Same Element |
| ---------------------------- | -------------- | -------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| `preventDefault()`           | ❌              | ✅                    | ❌                                    |
| `stopPropagation()`          | ✅              | ❌                    | ❌                                    |
| `stopImmediatePropagation()` | ✅              | ❌                    | ✅                                    |

***

# Senior React Interview Answer

```text
preventDefault()
→ Prevents default browser behaviour such as
  form submission or link navigation.

stopPropagation()
→ Stops the event from bubbling to parent
  elements.

stopImmediatePropagation()
→ Stops bubbling and prevents any additional
  handlers on the same element from executing.
```

### Easy Memory Trick

```text
preventDefault
= Stop Browser

stopPropagation
= Stop Parent

stopImmediatePropagation
= Stop Parent + Stop Siblings
```


# 1. Example with Multiple Handlers Using `stopImmediatePropagation()`

In React, `stopImmediatePropagation()` is available through the **native DOM event** (`e.nativeEvent`).

## Example

```jsx
import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";

export default function App() {
  const btnRef = useRef();

  useEffect(() => {
    const btn = btnRef.current;

    const handler1 = () => {
      console.log("Handler 1");
    };

    const handler2 = (e) => {
      console.log("Handler 2");

      e.stopImmediatePropagation();
    };

    const handler3 = () => {
      console.log("Handler 3");
    };

    btn.addEventListener("click", handler1);
    btn.addEventListener("click", handler2);
    btn.addEventListener("click", handler3);

    return () => {
      btn.removeEventListener("click", handler1);
      btn.removeEventListener("click", handler2);
      btn.removeEventListener("click", handler3);
    };
  }, []);

  return <button ref={btnRef}>Click Me</button>;
}
```

### Output

```text
Handler 1
Handler 2
```

```text
Handler 3 ❌
```

Because:

```javascript
stopImmediatePropagation()
```

stops:

✅ Remaining handlers on same element

✅ Further bubbling

***

# Real World React Example

Imagine:

```text
Analytics Handler
Audit Handler
Delete Handler
```

If Delete determines an action must be cancelled:

```javascript
e.nativeEvent.stopImmediatePropagation();
```

can prevent lower-priority handlers from running.

***

# 2. `preventDefault()` in Custom Form Validation

Most common React usage.

## Without preventDefault

```jsx
function LoginForm() {

  const handleSubmit = () => {
    console.log("Submit");
  };

  return (
    <form onSubmit={handleSubmit}>
      <button>Submit</button>
    </form>
  );
}
```

### What happens?

```text
Form Submitted
↓
Page Reloads
```

Browser performs default form action.

***

## With Custom Validation

```jsx
function LoginForm() {

  const handleSubmit = (e) => {

    e.preventDefault();

    const email = "";

    if (!email) {

      alert(
        "Email Required"
      );

      return;
    }

    console.log(
      "Valid Form"
    );
  };

  return (
    <form
      onSubmit={handleSubmit}
    >

      <input
        placeholder="Email"
      />

      <button>
        Submit
      </button>

    </form>
  );
}
```

***

### Flow

```text
Submit Click
      ↓
preventDefault()
      ↓
Validation
      ↓
API Call
      ↓
State Update
```

Instead of:

```text
Submit
↓
Browser Reload
```

***

## Interview Example

```jsx
const handleSubmit =
  async (e) => {

    e.preventDefault();

    if (!isValid()) {
      return;
    }

    await loginUser();
  };
```

Used in:

```text
✅ Login Forms
✅ Registration Forms
✅ Payment Forms
✅ Search Forms
✅ Multi-Step Wizards
```

***

# 3. `stopPropagation()` vs `stopImmediatePropagation()`

This is a favourite senior-level JavaScript interview question.

***

## stopPropagation()

Stops movement to parent elements.

### Example

```jsx
<div
  onClick={() =>
    console.log("Parent")
  }
>
  <button
    onClick={(e) => {

      e.stopPropagation();

      console.log("Child");
    }}
  >
    Click
  </button>
</div>
```

### Output

```text
Child
```

### Effect

```text
Child ✅
Parent ❌
```

***

## stopImmediatePropagation()

Stops:

```text
Child Handlers
Parent Handlers
Remaining Handlers
```

### Example

```javascript
button.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () => {
    console.log("A");
  }
);

button.addEventListener(
  "click",
  (e) => {

    console.log("B");

    e.stopImmediatePropagation();
  }
);

button.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () => {
    console.log("C");
  }
);
```

### Output

```text
A
B
```

```text
C ❌
```

***

# Visual Comparison

## stopPropagation()

```text
Child
  ↑
Parent
  ↑
GrandParent

Stops Here
```

Result:

```text
Child ✅
Parent ❌
```

***

## stopImmediatePropagation()

```text
Current Element

Handler1 ✅
Handler2 ✅

Stop

Handler3 ❌
Parent ❌
```

***

# Comparison Table

| Feature                             | stopPropagation() | stopImmediatePropagation() | preventDefault() |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------- | -------------------------- | ---------------- |
| Stop Bubbling                       | ✅                 | ✅                          | ❌                |
| Stop Parent Handlers                | ✅                 | ✅                          | ❌                |
| Stop Other Handlers on Same Element | ❌                 | ✅                          | ❌                |
| Stop Browser Default Action         | ❌                 | ❌                          | ✅                |
| Common in React                     | ✅ Very Common     | ⚠️ Rare                    | ✅ Very Common    |

***

# Senior React Interview Answer

```text
preventDefault()
→ Prevents browser behaviour such as
  form submission or link navigation.

stopPropagation()
→ Prevents an event from bubbling
  to ancestor elements.

stopImmediatePropagation()
→ Prevents bubbling and also stops
  any remaining event listeners on
  the current element from executing.
```

### Quick Memory Trick

```text
preventDefault()
= Stop Browser

stopPropagation()
= Stop Parent

stopImmediatePropagation()
= Stop Parent + Stop Sibling Handlers
```
